questions.prompt: Complete: "The children ___ in the street when it started to snow."
questions.explanation: "The children" is plural, so the past continuous uses were: they were playing when the snow started. "played" (past simple) loses the in-progress sense, "was playing" is the wrong agreement for a plural subject, and "are playing" is present.

questions.prompt: Complete the negative: "They ___ dinner; the kitchen was completely empty."
questions.explanation: The past-continuous negative for they is weren't + -ing: they weren't cooking. "wasn't cooking" is the wrong agreement (was is for I/he/she/it), "weren't cook" drops the -ing, and "didn't cooking" mixes two tenses incorrectly.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct -ing spelling: "She was ___ in her favourite armchair."
questions.explanation: sit ends in a short vowel + single consonant, so we double the t before -ing: sitting. "siting" keeps only one t, "sittting" adds an extra t, and "satting" starts from the past form sat — none are correct.

questions.prompt: Complete: "He ___ me when I arrived — he was looking at his phone."
questions.explanation: see is a stative verb and is not used in the continuous. For the short, completed event of not noticing someone arrive, use the past simple: he didn't see me. "wasn't seeing" and "was not seeing" wrongly put a state verb into the continuous, and "weren't seeing" also has wrong agreement.

questions.prompt: Complete: "___ they talking quietly so they answered without waking the baby?"
questions.explanation: A past-continuous question with they inverts subject and auxiliary: Were they talking…? "Was" is the wrong agreement for they, "While" starts a clause not a question, and "Did" forms the past-simple question and doesn't fit the continuous -ing that follows.

options.text: This time yesterday, we were flying over the Alps.
This time yesterday, we was flying over the Alps.
This time yesterday, we were fly over the Alps.
This time yesterday, we were flying over the Alps when we arrived.

questions.explanation: "This time yesterday" describes an action in progress at a past moment, so we use the past continuous: we were flying. "was" is the wrong agreement for we, "were fly" drops the -ing, and the last option adds "when we arrived" which contradicts being mid-flight over the Alps — the clause is inconsistent.
